choices.prompt: In Azure Data Factory, what is a pipeline as the module defines it?
choices: A directed graph of activities connected by dependency conditions
A single unit of work such as running one notebook or stored procedure
The integration runtime that decides where copy and transform compute runs
A JSON linked service that stores connection credentials
correct_answers: A directed graph of activities connected by dependency conditions

choices.prompt: The nightly Greenfork pipeline requires the supplier-shred stored procedure to run only if the sales-cleaning notebook finished cleanly, so that a bad Spark run never feeds half-clean data into the warehouse. Which dependency condition should the stored-procedure activity use on the notebook activity?
choices: Succeeded
Completion
Failed
Skipped

choices.prompt: A vendor drops a file into blob storage at an unpredictable time each night, and the pipeline must start the moment the file lands rather than guessing at a clock time. Which trigger type fits this arrival-driven requirement?
choices: An event-based (storage) trigger that fires on blob creation
A schedule trigger set to a wall-clock recurrence
A tumbling window trigger for fixed contiguous time slices
A self-hosted integration runtime configured to poll the lake
correct_answers: An event-based (storage) trigger that fires on blob creation

choices.prompt: According to the module, which statements correctly distinguish commit from publish in a Git-integrated Data Factory setup? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Commit captures intent and history in Git on a working branch
Publish deploys the collaboration branch to the running live service
Publishing without Git gives you no history, no review, and no rollback
Publish is just a save button that automatically records change history
correct_answers: Commit captures intent and history in Git on a working branch
Publish deploys the collaboration branch to the running live service
Publishing without Git gives you no history, no review, and no rollback

choices.prompt: Each nightly run owns a specific, contiguous time slice, and on occasion you must replay missed slices (backfill) with dependency between windows. Compared with a plain schedule trigger, which trigger type does the module recommend for these semantics, and why?
choices: A tumbling window trigger, because it tracks state and supports backfill and dependency between windows
A schedule trigger, because wall-clock recurrence already guarantees every slice is replayable
An event-based (storage) trigger, because slice ownership is decided by file arrival
An Execute Pipeline control activity, because it loops over each slice automatically
correct_answers: A tumbling window trigger, because it tracks state and supports backfill and dependency between windows

choices.prompt: The module describes several control activities that give a pipeline real logic. Which control activity should you use to iterate over a collection, such as looping over every store's file?
choices: ForEach
If Condition
Until
Execute Pipeline

choices.prompt: According to the module, what distinguishes a self-hosted integration runtime from the Azure (auto-resolve) integration runtime?
choices: A self-hosted IR reaches into a private network or on-premises source, while the Azure (auto-resolve) IR handles cloud-to-cloud movement and transforms
A self-hosted IR commits pipeline JSON to Git, while the Azure IR publishes it to the live service
A self-hosted IR fires the pipeline on blob arrival, while the Azure IR fires it on a wall-clock recurrence
A self-hosted IR computes pipeline parameters from the trigger time, while the Azure IR hardcodes them
correct_answers: A self-hosted IR reaches into a private network or on-premises source, while the Azure (auto-resolve) IR handles cloud-to-cloud movement and transforms

choices.prompt: Rather than hardcoding a date into the pipeline, the nightly Greenfork pipeline computes the run date at trigger time. Which expressions and constructs does the module present for parameterizing and computing dates in this way? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Referencing a pipeline parameter with @pipeline().parameters.runDate
Using the system variable @pipeline().TriggerTime
Formatting a date with @formatDateTime(...)
Embedding a literal value such as 2026-06-12 directly in the activity setting

choices.prompt: In the walkthrough JSON, the notebook activity carries a policy of retry 2 with retryIntervalInSeconds 300. According to the module, what is the purpose of this retry policy?
choices: A transient cluster hiccup retries twice before failing the pipeline, so a temporary failure does not kill the whole run
It guarantees the downstream stored procedure runs even if the notebook ultimately fails
It backfills any missed nightly time slices by replaying them automatically
It commits the activity's JSON to the Git working branch after each attempt
correct_answers: A transient cluster hiccup retries twice before failing the pipeline, so a temporary failure does not kill the whole run

choices.prompt: The module's pitfalls warn against using 'Completion' where you meant 'Succeeded'. Which statements about these dependency conditions are correct per the module? (Select all that apply.)
choices: A dependency on Completion runs the next activity on success or failure, so it can silently load bad data when misused
Completion should be reserved for cleanup activities, while Succeeded is used for the happy path
A Completion dependency runs the next activity only when the upstream activity failed
Succeeded and Completion are interchangeable and produce identical run behavior
correct_answers: A dependency on Completion runs the next activity on success or failure, so it can silently load bad data when misused
Completion should be reserved for cleanup activities, while Succeeded is used for the happy path

llm.prompt: Explain why using a dependency condition of 'Completion' instead of 'Succeeded' between the sales-cleaning notebook activity and the supplier-shred stored procedure is dangerous, and describe the correct way to encode the 'do B only if A worked' contract in the nightly Greenfork pipeline.
llm.reference_answer: A dependency condition of 'Completion' runs the downstream activity whether the upstream one succeeded or failed, so if the Spark notebook fails the stored procedure still runs and silently loads bad or half-clean data into the warehouse. To encode 'do B only if A worked', the stored-procedure activity's dependsOn block should set the dependencyConditions to 'Succeeded' on the Clean_Sales_Spark activity, so the load runs only after a successful upstream completion. This is the load-bearing line of the pipeline: dependency conditions encode the success/failure contract between activities declaratively, without writing control-flow code. 'Completion' should be reserved for cleanup activities that must run on success or failure, and 'Failed' for alerts.

llm.prompt: Compare the three trigger types the module describes (schedule, tumbling window, and event-based) and walk through how you would choose among them for a nightly batch, naming the semantics each one implies.
llm.reference_answer: A schedule trigger fires on a wall-clock recurrence, for example every night at 02:00, giving clock-driven semantics that suit a fixed nightly window. A tumbling window trigger fires for fixed, contiguous, non-overlapping time slices and, importantly, tracks state and supports backfill and dependency between windows, so it is the right choice when each run owns a specific time slice and you may need to replay missed slices. An event-based (storage) trigger fires when a blob is created or deleted, giving arrival-driven semantics so the pipeline starts the moment the vendor's file lands instead of guessing at a time. To choose, you pick the semantics of your batch: clock-driven for a plain nightly recurrence, slice-driven when slices and backfill matter, and arrival-driven when the start should be tied to a file landing. Using a plain schedule trigger when each run owns a backfillable slice is a common pitfall because it costs you replay capability.

llm.prompt: A teammate published a pipeline change directly to the live service, it broke production, and there was no way to roll back. Walk through how connecting the factory to a Git repository changes this workflow and why it provides history, review, and rollback that publish-only cannot.
llm.reference_answer: By default, changes saved by publishing go straight to the Data Factory service with no history, no review, and no rollback. Connecting the factory to a Git repository (Azure Repos or GitHub) means your edits are committed to a working branch as JSON artifacts (pipelines, datasets, linked services, triggers), so you open a pull request, a teammate reviews the diff, you merge to the collaboration branch, and only then publish to the live service from that branch. Because the artifacts are just JSON, they diff and review like any code, giving a review gate before deployment. If a change breaks production you can revert the offending commit and republish, restoring a known-good state. The key distinction is that commit captures intent and history in Git while publish deploys the collaboration branch to the running service, so publish should be treated as a deployment step, not a save button.
